name: El Vidré and Xodos Pass Loop from Atzeneta
name_original: Los puertos de Vistabella y Xodos — Circular desde Atzeneta del Maestrat
description: The El Vidré and Xodos Pass Loop from Atzeneta is a difficult 33.5-mile road cycling route with 4552 feet of climbing.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Atzeneta del Maestrat traverse a varied landscape at the foot of the Penyagolosa massif, offering diverse terrain from agricultural fields to mountainous areas. The region features numerous mountain passes, including the Coll de la Bandereta and Xodos Pass, known for their consistent gradients and wide asphalt roads. Cyclists encounter views of the Maestrat and Plana Alta, with routes often winding through olive and almond groves. The area's topography provides options for both moderate and challenging road cycling experiences. Best road cycling routes around Atzeneta del Maestrat The most popular road cycling route is El Vidré and Xodos Pass Loop from Atzeneta, a 33.5 miles (53.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 12 minutes to complete. This route features scenic mountain passes with consistent gradients. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Tour of Plana Alta — Circular from Atzeneta del Maestrat, a difficult 57.8 miles (93.0 km) path. It winds through the diverse landscapes of the Maestrat and Plana Alta regions. Local road cyclists also love the View of Ares del Maestre – Quiet Country Road loop from Atzeneta del Maestrat, a 62.2 miles (100.1 km) trail leading through picturesque mountain passes and expansive views, often completed in about 5 hours 6 minutes. Road cycling around Atzeneta del Maestrat is defined by challenging mountain passes, agricultural fields, and views of the Penyagolosa massif. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Atzeneta del Maestrat are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 180 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Atzeneta del Maestrat's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Atzeneta del Maestrat?
A: There are over 10 road cycling routes around Atzeneta del Maestrat, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. You'll find options ranging from moderate to difficult.

Q: What are the typical difficulty levels for road cycling routes in this region?
A: The road cycling routes in Atzeneta del Maestrat primarily range from moderate to difficult. There are 5 moderate routes and 9 difficult routes listed, featuring significant elevation gains and challenging mountain passes.

Q: Are there any beginner-friendly or easy road cycling routes in Atzeneta del Maestrat?
A: While many routes are challenging, the region does offer options for beginners. For instance, the "Master's Atzeneta" is a 38-kilometer path with a moderate elevation gain, providing comfortable roads through the Maestrat and Plana Alta landscapes. Another accessible option is a route from Tirig, featuring gentle slopes through almond and olive groves.

Q: What kind of terrain and elevation gain can I expect on road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling in Atzeneta del Maestrat features diverse terrain, from agricultural fields with almond and olive trees to mountainous areas at the foot of the Penyagolosa massif. You can expect significant elevation gains, with routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e881931279/el-vidre-and-xodos-pass-loop-from-atzeneta" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Vidré and Xodos Pass Loop from Atzeneta&lt;/a&gt; climbing over 1,300 meters, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24539726" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Ares del Maestre – Quiet Country Road loop from Atzeneta del Maestrat&lt;/a&gt; reaching over 1,600 meters.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, Atzeneta del Maestrat is ideal for experienced cyclists seeking a challenge. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e883506173/tour-of-plana-alta-circular-from-atzeneta-del-maestrat" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour of Plana Alta — Circular from Atzeneta del Maestrat&lt;/a&gt; cover nearly 93 km with over 1,300 meters of ascent. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38180673" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Atzeneta del Maestrat – Vistabella del Maestrat loop&lt;/a&gt; is another demanding option, featuring over 1,200 meters of elevation gain over 52 km.

Q: What scenic viewpoints or natural features can I expect to see while road cycling?
A: The region offers stunning views of the Maestrat and Plana Alta landscapes. You can cycle through picturesque mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3410303" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll de la Bandereta&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3890802" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Xodos / Chodos Pass (Atzeneta Side)&lt;/a&gt;. The area is also at the foot of the Penyagolosa massif, with the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839679" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Penyagolosa Summit&lt;/a&gt; offering panoramic vistas. You'll encounter diverse terrain, from agricultural fields to pine forests and rocky landscapes.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or attractions near the road cycling routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can explore historical sites. The town of Atzeneta del Maestrat itself has a historic center with structures like the Torre de la Prisión and the Castillo de Atzeneta. Archaeological sites, including an Iberian settlement near the Ermita de San Juan, and caves like Cova dels Aragonesos, add to the area's appeal.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in Atzeneta del Maestrat are circular. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e881931279/el-vidre-and-xodos-pass-loop-from-atzeneta" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Vidré and Xodos Pass Loop from Atzeneta&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e883506173/tour-of-plana-alta-circular-from-atzeneta-del-maestrat" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour of Plana Alta — Circular from Atzeneta del Maestrat&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17509280" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Atzeneta del Maestrat – Atzeneta Pass via Les Useres loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Atzeneta del Maestrat?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. Cyclists often praise the challenging mountain passes, the diverse landscapes of agricultural fields and mountainous terrain, and the expansive views, particularly of the Penyagolosa massif.

Q: What are some of the prominent mountain passes for road cycling in the area?
A: Atzeneta del Maestrat is known for its scenic mountain passes. Key passes include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3410303" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll de la Bandereta&lt;/a&gt;, considered one of the most picturesque in La Plana Alta, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3890802" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Xodos / Chodos Pass (Atzeneta Side)&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a wide asphalt road with consistent gradients. Another notable pass is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3890819" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll de la Bassa (Atzeneta Side)&lt;/a&gt;, featuring tree-lined slopes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Atzeneta del Maestrat?
A: While specific seasonal recommendations are not provided, the diverse landscapes and varied difficulty levels suggest that spring and autumn would offer pleasant temperatures for cycling. Summer might be suitable for early morning or late afternoon rides to avoid the midday heat, especially on more exposed routes.
tips:
https://www.dandolotodo09.com/2018/12/puertos-el-collao-vidre-por-vistabella.html

In the classic cycling stage of going up to Vistabella (a stone's throw from the sky), you always have to return to Atzeneta tasting the Vidre from behind. Fortunately, the north face is much shorter than the main south slope that climbs from Atzeneta. On the north face we find a port of just over 3 km, with constant slopes and some hairpin bends. It tends to be harder than it really is because one always arrives cold after the Vistabella break. The nomenclature of the port was always the Vidre, it has recently been renamed Port del Collao, as the new plaques attest. Formerly there was a plaque with a graffiti that marked it as Coll de la Xaparra, because near the top there is a Farmhouse and a ravine with the same name.

https://www.dandolotodo09.com/2018/12/puertos-vistabella-por-el-vidre-apm-50.html

The ascent to the highest town in the entire Valencian Community occupies a prominent place among the list of ports that the province offers. For a few years now, the climb has new asphalt and shiny glass when the sun bathes the road. It is a very constant port, it does not have large ramps, and in the case of wanting to drop your fellow travelers, it is best to mature them in the long previous port (El Coll del Vidre) and finish them off in the first ramps of the Vistabella port. The confusion with the nomenclature of the Vidre and Vistabella ports. To get to the port of Vistabella, you have to climb the previous port of 13 km from Atzeneta. Until a few years ago, the long port was always known as the Vidre, even as the Coll de la Xaparra (a graffiti on the top testified to this). And the port that concerns us today was always known as the port of Vistabella. Well, now there are new plates on the tops and new names. What used to be the Vidre, now the Collao port. And what was Vistabella is now labeled as the Coll del Vidre. Despite the changes...I'm reluctant to use the new names, I prefer the old ones. Going up to eat at Vistabella in the spring or in the fall...it is surely one of the greatest pleasures that a true cyclist can enjoy.

https://www.dandolotodo09.com/2021/09/puertos-xodos-chodos-por-atzeneta-apm.html

The port that goes up to Xodos/Chodos is one of the great colossi of the province of Castellón, with its more than 16 kilometers in length and almost 700 meters of accumulated ascent. We have waited a long time to visit it, but the ascent was worth it. Our journey begins in the town of Atzeneta del Maestrat. Very wide asphalt road in perfect condition, a paradise for bikers who trace the curves as if they were on a circuit. Shadows there is not one. It is the port of the 5 peaks, which gives an idea of what breaks we do have during its ascent. Between peaks 2 and 3 we have 5 very constant kilometers, where the slope rarely drops below 6%. Speaking of slopes, the maximum is around 10-11% and is reached at various points in the port. Arriving at the town of Xodos, the landscape opens up and we can contemplate one of the most beautiful pictures of the province, with the town perched on a cliff and the Gegant de Pedra el Penyagolosa to its left. To reach the highest point (1,103 meters above sea level) you have to cross the town and climb almost 1 kilometer more along a narrower and somewhat rougher road. The other side of the port runs along the aforementioned road and ends at the beginning of the Port of Vistabella, which is now called Coll del Vidre on the plates.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to cycle the El Vidré and Xodos Pass Loop?
faqs.answer: This challenging racebike route usually takes around 3 hours and 12 minutes to complete. However, your actual time may vary depending on your fitness level, speed, and how often you stop to enjoy the views.

faqs.question: Is the El Vidré and Xodos Pass Loop suitable for beginner cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult and requires a very good level of fitness. It involves significant elevation gains, including two long and demanding climbs up the Port del Collao (Coll del Vidre) and the Puerto de Xodos, with gradients reaching up to 10-11%.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The loop typically starts and ends in Atzeneta del Maestrat. You should be able to find suitable parking within the town to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily follows wide and well-paved roads, which are ideal for race biking. While the climbs are demanding, the road surfaces are generally smooth, allowing for efficient ascents and descents.

faqs.question: What are the main highlights or viewpoints along the El Vidré and Xodos Pass Loop?
faqs.answer: You will encounter several notable mountain passes and stunning views. Key highlights include the challenging climbs over Port del Collao (Coll del Vidre) and Xodos / Chodos Pass (Atzeneta Side). Near Xodos, you'll be rewarded with dramatic views of the village perched on a cliff, with the imposing Penyagolosa mountain in the background. The route also passes through traditional olive and almond groves.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during the spring or autumn months. The weather is generally milder and more pleasant for demanding climbs, avoiding the intense heat of summer or the colder conditions of winter at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other official cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant portions of the [CIMA CS04] El Vidré - Vistabella * Azteneta del Maestrat (53%) and [CIMA CS09] Xodos (35%) routes. You will also briefly encounter parts of El Camí IT6TR1 El Camí Central: de Castelló de la Plana a Montserrat and Antic Camí Real.

faqs.question: Is the El Vidré and Xodos Pass Loop dog-friendly for cyclists?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally welcome in the region, road cycling with a dog on a leash can be challenging and potentially unsafe on a route with significant climbs and descents. It is not typically recommended for this type of demanding racebike tour.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for cycling in Parc Natural de Penyagolosa?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Parc Natural de Penyagolosa. However, always respect local regulations, especially regarding protected areas, and stay on designated routes.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Atzeneta del Maestrat, your starting point, and the village of Xodos offer options for refreshments and accommodation. Atzeneta has several establishments like El Molí Glamping, El Mangranar, and Casa Ramón. Xodos also provides local amenities for cyclists.

faqs.question: What essential gear should I bring for this challenging road cycling trip?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, ensure you bring plenty of water and nutrition, a repair kit (spare tubes, pump), appropriate cycling attire for varying temperatures, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. A GPS device or a downloaded Komoot map is highly recommended for navigation.
